OK, I've had a look into this and it's ok, the `Subreddit` object you get from `Submission.subreddit` is not "fetched" and has been initialised with the subreddit name and that is all that is returned from `Subreddit.__str__()`.

A lot of the calls on the `Subreddit` object *will* cause the cache to be refreshed (or initialised) but the subreddit name is not one of them. `__str__()` also does not cause the cache to be updated as it's just returning the name of the Sub, which is a param to `__init__()` passed to it by the `Submission` object.

In short, this is fine, but I understand your hesitance.

morf = module or file. Namespace is a legitimate module and when encountered the coverage API does neither ignore it nor refuses to process it in a coherent manner - it blows up.

> Attempting to profile a raw Module object isn't very indicative - and in fact modules without files are legitimate things - e.g. dynamically compiled code - but you can still get their source, to be able to report on them.

This code will fail completely not only on Namespace but on every morf that has no `__file__` attribute AND is not a string. By [here](https://github.com/nedbat/coveragepy/pull/1/files#diff-684b48b57362eec44f310362ea6376abL101) you already have no chance of recovering from a module morf without a __file__ attribute.

If you're in favor of more extensive changes I'm all for them, but *de minimis* if coverage cannot accept modules not backed by a file it should at least not blow up unintelligibly.

Instead of using this line, which is in effect a duplication of [line 147](https://github.com/pipermerriam/flex/pull/59/files#diff-79b7810358d612d76f53cf572cbc0f20R147), I think it'd be better to remove this line, and replace line 147 with the following.

```python
matching_api_paths = [(p, r.match(target_path)) for p, r in paths.items() if r.match(target_path)]
```
Now that `matching_api_paths` will be a list of 2-tuples of (*api_path*, *path_regex_match*), all of the places where `matching_api_paths` is accessed will need to be changed accordingly.

- [line 166](https://github.com/pipermerriam/flex/pull/59/files#diff-79b7810358d612d76f53cf572cbc0f20R166) will need to change to something like `[v[0] for v in matching_api_paths]` in order to get at the inner API paths and not dump a bunch of regex matches into the error message.
- [line 170](https://github.com/pipermerriam/flex/pull/59/files#diff-79b7810358d612d76f53cf572cbc0f20R170) will need to change to `return matching_api_paths[0][0]` to get at the api_path in the inner tuple.


Once those are cleaned up, we can remove the `matches` variable and just use `matching_api_paths` in its place.

As far as I understand, this is the part where backtracking happens. It's not the pattern itself that goes back, it's the `.search`, which makes python retry the `[^<]*`match starting from each of the string characters:

But since our pattern starts with a wildcard match and we rerun it after matching a tag from the end of previous match I think we can replace this (and the repeated call within the loop) with `.match`, which will only try to apply the pattern from the beginning of the string. But I might be missing something about why we need `.search` there.

```
In [24]: %timeit re.search(r'([^<]*)(<!--.*?--\s*>|<[^>]*>)', 'hello'*2000)
1.02 s ± 71.1 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

In [25]: %timeit re.match(r'([^<]*)(<!--.*?--\s*>|<[^>]*>)', 'hello'*2000)
202 µs ± 4.92 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)

# This doesn't happen when there is a match

In [26]: %timeit re.match(r'([^<]*)(<!--.*?--\s*>|<[^>]*>)', 'hello<tag>'*2000)
2.34 µs ± 214 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100000 loops each)

In [27]: %timeit re.search(r'([^<]*)(<!--.*?--\s*>|<[^>]*>)', 'hello<tag>'*2000)
2.17 µs ± 113 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100000 loops each)
```

Can you write down here whether the functionality here tells you the literal value in the BUILD file, or tells you the value that pants will actually use, or something else? Each of these options are potentially useful, but noting which one we expect is handy.

For instance, in the following:

```
dir$ cat BUILD
java_library(
  dependencies = [
    "//foo:bar",
    "//baz:bash",
  ],
)

dir$ ls -1
BUILD
Foo.java
```

there are a bunch of things which Pants will internally do which aren't seen in the BUILD file. Some examples:

1. The `name` attribute will be populated with the name of the directory containing the BUILD file. Say the directory is named "dir"; should peek return "dir", "", some special value which denote "missing", some special value which denotes "computed", or an error?
2. The `srcs` attribute will automatically be expanded to `globs("*.java")`. Should peek error / return "" (because it's absent), return `globs("*.java")` because that's what's in the BUILD file, or call the function `globs` and return the list of java files which it actually matches (i.e. `["Foo.java"]`)?
3. Ordering isn't significant in the dependencies attribute, and Pants internally will sort the `dependencies` attribute. Should we return the unsorted list (because that's what in the BUILD file), or a sorted version (because that's what Pants will actually use, and it makes comparing two targets easier)?

@wisechengyi I don't know what you're expecting the answers to these questions to be either, or whether you've had this discussion already :)

Here’s how the weird magic works. A grouping is represented by a minimum code point, a maximum code point, and a bitfield of which code points in that range are in the grouping. For example, in english.sbl, `valid_LI` is `'cdeghkmnrt'`. The minimum and maximum code points are 99 and 116, so every time one of these grouping functions is called for `g_valid_LI`, `min` must be 99 and `max` must be 116. There are 18 code points within that range (inclusive) so the grouping is represented with 18 bits, plus 6 bits of padding:
```
cdefghij klmnopqr st
11101100 10110001 01000000
```
The first bit is the least significant. Those three bytes become `&[0b00110111, 0b10001101, 0b00000010]`, which is `&[55, 141, 2]`, which is how `g_valid_LI` is defined in english.rs.

Let's say the user wants to call `AbstractDockerContainer.logs` (or
`DockerHubContainer.logs`) on several containers. The client could put together
a request that looks like this:

```python
lost_list = [
    container.logs(data={'tail': 50, 'stdout': True, 'stderr': False})
    for container in containers
]
```

This works just fine. Another way to write the above is this:

```python
data = {'tail': 50, 'stdout': True, 'stderr': False}
lost_list = [container.logs(data=data) for container in containers]
```

In this latter form, we depend upon `data` being the same for every single
request. Right? It would be pretty weird if we passed `data` in to a method and
the method altered `data`. In fact, if the method altered `data`, that would
break the latter way of writing our code!

`kwargs.copy()` prevents `data` from being altered. Check this out:

```python
>>> def logs(data):
...     data.update({'tail': 1000})
... 
>>> data = {'tail': 50, 'stdout': True, 'stderr': False}
>>> logs(data)
>>> data
{'tail': 1000, 'stderr': False, 'stdout': True}
```

Uh oh! Using `kwargs.copy()` avoid this issue:

```python
>>> def logs(data):
...     data = data.copy()  # shadow the passed-in argument
...     data.update({'tail': 1000})
... 
>>> data = {'tail': 50, 'stdout': True, 'stderr': False}
>>> logs(data)
>>> data
{'tail': 50, 'stderr': False, 'stdout': True}
```

# perform codegen for one language/API combo

>Use long and descriptive names for testing functions. The style guide here is slightly different than that of running code, where short names are often preferred. the main issue was just that `TOWER_SCOPE` in `multi_gpu_impl` would introduce a different scope, where variables then could not be reused from the local evaluator. 

Basically, there are three places where the policy graph is built: 
 - the local evaluator
 - the `shared_loss` in the `par_opt`
 - the `device_loss`es in the `par_opt`

`tf.get_variable_scope().reuse_variables()` gets the scope where the local_evaluator copy resides, but the `TOWER_SCOPE_NAME` gates off the `par_opt` copies. 

On a separate branch right now, I tried making the local evaluator and `shared_loss` the same copy, but it doesn't work yet and I think I need an extra sync between the `device_losses` graph and the local evaluator graph...
